findings.message: production Go file is 801 lines (> 600)
findings.why_it_matters: Very large files are hard to review, encourage dumping-ground growth, and hide AI residue.
findings.remediation: Split the file into smaller, cohesive units or move unrelated logic out.

findings.message: review go statement ownership and shutdown behavior
findings.why_it_matters: Background goroutines are a common source of hidden lifecycle and reliability drift.
findings.remediation: Confirm the goroutine has an owner, a stop path, and panic handling.

findings.message: production Go file is 463 lines (warning range 401-600)
findings.why_it_matters: Large files are refactor hotspots even when they are not yet blocking.
findings.remediation: Consider splitting the file before it grows past the blocking threshold.

findings.message: avoid new log.Printf calls in production code
findings.why_it_matters: Ad hoc printing bypasses structured logging and makes operational diagnostics inconsistent.
findings.remediation: Use the repository's structured logging helpers instead of direct fmt/log printing.

findings.message: avoid disallowed filename token "util"
findings.why_it_matters: Repository naming guardrails keep AI-generated catch-all names out of the codebase.
findings.remediation: Rename the file to a domain-specific name.

findings.message: avoid new Background() in production code
findings.why_it_matters: Background contexts hide cancellation and timeout boundaries in production code.
findings.remediation: Thread an existing context into this call or document a reviewed entrypoint exception in the allowlist.

findings.message: review panic usage in production code
findings.why_it_matters: Panics in production paths can turn recoverable failures into process-wide incidents.
findings.remediation: Prefer explicit error propagation unless this is a deliberately fatal bootstrap path.
